content:
# Deuteronomy Chapter 9 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 104r–105v | **Source Images:** DEU_009_page_001.jpg, DEU_009_page_002.jpg, DEU_009_vaticanus_page_001.jpg, DEU_009_vaticanus_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Listen, Israel: today you are about to cross the Jordan and go in to drive out nations greater and more powerful than you — great cities with walls towering to the sky —

**2** a people great and tall, the Anakim, whom you know about, of whom you have heard it said, "Who can stand against the Anakim?"

**3** But understand this: the LORD your God himself goes before you as a consuming fire. He will destroy them; he will bring them down before you. You will drive them out and finish them off quickly, just as the LORD has promised you.

**4** After the LORD your God drives them out before you, do not say to yourself, "It was because of my righteousness that the LORD brought me in to take this land." No — it is because of the wickedness of these nations that the LORD is driving them out before you.

**5** It is not because of your righteousness or the integrity of your heart that you are going in to take their land — it is because of the wickedness of these nations that the LORD your God is driving them out before you, and in order to fulfill the word he swore to your ancestors, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob.

**6** Understand this: it is not because of your righteousness that the LORD your God is giving you this good land to possess — for you are a stiff-necked people.

**7** Remember this and never forget: how you provoked the LORD your God to fury in the wilderness. From the day you left Egypt until you arrived here, you have been rebellious against the LORD.

**8** At Horeb you provoked the LORD to wrath so intense that he was ready to destroy you.

**9** When I went up the mountain to receive the stone tablets — the tablets of the covenant the LORD made with you — I stayed on the mountain forty days and forty nights, eating no bread and drinking no water.

**10** The LORD gave me the two stone tablets, written with the finger of God, and on them were inscribed all the words the LORD had spoken to you on the mountain out of the fire on the day of the assembly.

**11** At the end of forty days and forty nights, the LORD handed me the two stone tablets — the tablets of the covenant.

**12** Then the LORD said to me, "Get up — go down from here quickly, because your people, whom you brought out of Egypt, have corrupted themselves. They have turned off the path I commanded them so quickly and have made a cast idol for themselves."

**13** And the LORD said to me, "I have seen this people — they are a stiff-necked people indeed.

**14** Leave me alone, so that I may destroy them and wipe out their name from under the heavens, and I will make of you a nation stronger and more numerous than they."

**15** So I turned and came down from the mountain while it was still blazing with fire. The two tablets of the covenant were in both my hands.

**16** I looked, and there you were — you had sinned against the LORD your God! You had made yourselves a cast calf idol. You had turned aside so quickly from the path the LORD had commanded you!

**17** I grabbed the two tablets and hurled them from my hands, shattering them before your eyes.

**18** Then I threw myself down before the LORD, as I had done the first time — forty days and forty nights — eating no bread and drinking no water — because of all the sin you had committed by doing what is evil in the LORD's sight, arousing his fury.

**19** For I was terrified by the LORD's fierce anger — the blazing wrath that threatened to destroy you. But the LORD listened to me that time also.

**20** The LORD was so furious with Aaron that he was ready to destroy him. So I prayed for Aaron at that time as well.

**21** As for your sin — that calf you had made — I took it and burned it in the fire, crushed it and ground it to fine dust, and threw the dust into the stream that flowed down from the mountain.

**22** You also provoked the LORD to fury at Taberah, at Massah, and at Kibroth-hattaavah.

**23** And when the LORD sent you out from Kadesh-barnea, saying, "Go up and take possession of the land I am giving you" — you rebelled against the command of the LORD your God. You did not trust him. You did not obey his voice.

**24** You have been rebellious against the LORD from the very first day I knew you.

**25** I lay prostrate before the LORD for those forty days and forty nights because the LORD had said he would destroy you.

**26** And I prayed to the LORD: "O Lord GOD, do not destroy your people, your very own inheritance, whom you redeemed through your greatness and brought out of Egypt with a mighty hand.

**27** Remember your servants Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob. Do not look at the stubbornness of this people, their wickedness, and their sin —

**28** or the land you brought us from will say, 'The LORD was not able to bring them into the land he promised them, and because he hated them, he brought them out to kill them in the wilderness.'

**29** But they are your people, your inheritance, whom you brought out by your great power and your outstretched arm."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 1 — "walls towering to the sky":** The Hebrew arim gedolot u-vetsuroth ba-shamayim ("great cities walled up to the heavens") is idiomatic hyperbole for massively fortified walls. PNEUMA renders "towering to the sky" to preserve the rhetorical awe while remaining natural in English. The LXX has poleis megales kai teticheismenas heos tou ouranou — the same sky-high image.

**V. 3 — "consuming fire" for esh okhlah:** This is one of the Bible's great theophanic images — God as fire that devours. PNEUMA retains "consuming fire" because it is both accurate and evocative; it also connects with Hebrews 12:29. The LXX reads pyr katanaliskkon (devouring fire).

**V. 4–6 — Triple refutation of self-righteousness:** Moses repeats the same idea three times: "not because of your righteousness." This is one of the most forceful theological arguments in Deuteronomy. PNEUMA keeps all three repetitions intact with slight variation in rendering — "my righteousness" (v.4), "your righteousness or the integrity of your heart" (v.5), "your righteousness" again (v.6) — matching the Hebrew's own variation. The LXX adds dikaiosynen (righteousness) as a legal-moral concept throughout.

**V. 6 — "stiff-necked" (qesheh oref):** Literally "hard of neck" — a metaphor from oxen refusing to bow to the yoke. PNEUMA preserves "stiff-necked" because it has become a recognized English idiom with exactly this biblical valence. The LXX uses sklerotrachelon, the Greek calque of the Hebrew.

**V. 12 — "they have corrupted themselves":** The Hebrew hishchitu — root sh-ch-t, meaning to corrupt, ruin, or destroy. Moses quotes God as saying "your people" (not "my people") — the narrative distancing mirrors the divine disgust. PNEUMA makes the shift explicit: "your people, whom you brought out," capturing God's pointed reassignment of ownership.

**V. 17 — "I grabbed the two tablets and hurled them":** The Hebrew vatishlak is forceful, dramatic — Moses is not slowly setting them down. PNEUMA preserves the violence of the gesture: grabbed, hurled, shattered. The LXX uses erripsa (I flung them). The smashing of the tablets is not presented as an accident but as a deliberate, passionate act of prophetic grief.

**V. 19 — "I was terrified by the LORD's fierce anger":** The Hebrew ki yagorti mipney ha-af — literally "I was afraid because of the anger and the hot fury." PNEUMA renders Moses' own terror explicitly: "I was terrified by the LORD's fierce anger." This honest confession of fear is often softened in translations; here it is the emotional engine of Moses' intercession.

**V. 26–29 — Moses' intercession as theology:** Moses' prayer inverts the logic of destruction. He appeals to: (1) the LORD's own investment — "your very own inheritance"; (2) the patriarchal covenant — Abraham, Isaac, Jacob; (3) the LORD's reputation among the nations — what will Egypt say? The LXX follows the MT closely. PNEUMA renders this as direct address — "O Lord GOD" (Adonai YHWH) — marking the solemnity. The prayer is Moses at his most priestly: standing between a holy God and a guilty people.

— Signed, PNEUMA
